headlineSummary: OpenAI and Google Gemini drew mostly positive comments with a close tie, while Anthropic landed neutral-to-mixed on a split set of reactions [E1][E6][E7][E3][E4][E5].

ranking.summary: Anthropic came across as mixed and neutral overall, with praise for Claude's help in testing balanced by criticism of Claude Code's behavior and trust issues [E2][E3][E4][E5].
OpenAI had a generally positive read, with praise for Codex 5.2's capability and its provenance metadata, though one comment mixed that with concern about a clever exploit [E1][E2][E7].
Google Gemini was viewed positively on speed and authentication features, but this is based on limited evidence and it is in a close tie with OpenAI [E6][E7].

evidence.excerpt: GPT-5.2 came up with a clever solution involving chaining 7 function calls through glibc’s exit handler mechanism. Yikes.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly credits GPT-5.2 with a clever exploit solution, implying strong capability, but immediately reacts with concern ('Yikes'), making the overall stance mixed.

evidence.excerpt: Opus 4.5 is my everyday driver - I like using it. But Codex 5.2 with Extra High thinking is just a bit more powerful.

annotations.rationale: The user says Opus 4.5 is their everyday driver and explicitly says they like using it, which is mild praise.
The comment says Codex 5.2 is 'a bit more powerful,' a favorable comparison that mildly praises its capability.

evidence.excerpt: I've found that with a bit of guidance, Claude is pretty good at suggesting interesting properties to test and writing property tests to verify that invariants hold.

annotations.rationale: This is straightforward mild praise for Claude's usefulness in suggesting and writing property tests.

evidence.excerpt: Going to provide a simple example with Claude Codes. I have a frontend agent instructed to not use any class or style ever, only the design system components and primitives. Not only it will ignore those very quickly, but when it proposes edits and I give feedback the agent ignores them completely and instead it keeps suggesting more edits.

annotations.rationale: The commenter gives a concrete failure case for Claude Code(s), saying it ignores instructions and feedback, which is clear criticism of its behavior.

evidence.excerpt: I have found even when I say a credential exists and is correct Claude does not believe me. Which is infuriating.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Claude does not believe them about valid credentials and calls that behavior infuriating, which is mild-to-moderate criticism.

evidence.excerpt: When I want fast I reach for Gemini, or Cerebras

annotations.rationale: The commenter says they choose Gemini when they want speed, indicating a favorable view of Gemini's performance.

evidence.excerpt: - OpenAI uses the C2PA standard [0] to add provenance metadata to images, which you can check [1] - Gemini uses SynthId [2] and adds a watermark to the image. The watermark can be removed, but SynthId cannot as it is part of the image. SynthId is used to watermark text as well, and code is open-source [3]

annotations.rationale: The commenter presents OpenAI's provenance metadata as one of the 'easy ways to verify' AI-generated images, which frames it as a useful safety/authenticity feature.
The comment cites Gemini's SynthId watermarking as a durable authenticity mechanism, presenting it as a helpful safety feature.
